title: Editorial: New Approaches to ­Research and Curatorial Practice
content:
Dr Kirsi Eskelinen, Director, Sinebrychoff Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery

June 17, 2026

This issue of FNG Research is rich in approaches to research into visual arts and curatorial practices. For example the Sinebrychoff Art Museum recently opened its themed exhibition, ‘Night’ which explores the night and the world of dreams, illuminated by old European art and works from the 19th century to the present. Besides the artworks, poetry also has a powerful presence in the exhibition. Curator Kersti Tainio decided to commission contemporary poet Henriikka Tavi to write poems specific to the themes in each room instead of using traditional wall texts. Rather than explaining this special time of day to visitors, they are encouraged to feel and interpret the night. Instead of a set of distinct works, the exhibition is a total work of art, with its architecture – devised by designer Lauri Johansson – also playing a major role. Gill Crabbe discusses the collaborative process with Henriikka Tavi in her article ‘A Night to Remember’.
snippet:
Dr Kirsi Eskelinen, Director, Sinebrychoff Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery

June 17, 2026

title: From Plaster and Marble to Lost-wax Bronze: The Materiality of Sculpture from the Helsinki Exhibition of 1876 to Early 20th-century Finland
content:
Mari Tossavainen, PhD, visiting scholar, University of Helsinki

—

This article examines sculptors’ material practices and major shifts in sculptural materials in Finland from the late 19th to the early 20th century, with particular attention to marble, plaster, and the lost-wax (cire perdue) method.[1] It contributes to ongoing discussions on sculptural materials and methods. Although materiality has long been central to the fields of conservation and restoration, a material perspective on sculpture has been largely neglected in studies of Finnish 19th- and early 20th-century sculpture.[2]

title: A Night to Remember
content:
Gill Crabbe, FNG Research

A themed exhibition at the Sinebrychoff Art Museum, Helsinki, on the subject of night, has taken an innovative approach in inviting a contemporary poet, Henriikka Tavi, to contribute. Gill Crabbe discusses the collaborative process with her

—

The advent of digital art museums that create immersive experiences from the works of iconic historical masters, such as van Gogh, has had a mixed reception among museum professionals. These new media spaces with their fast-paced moving projections onto walls, ceilings and floors, entwined with intense sensory soundtracks, are a far cry from the unhurried contemplation encouraged in more traditional exhibition formats. So how does a museum like the Finnish National Gallery’s Sinebrychoff Art Museum, which showcases old masterworks and art from the Renaissance to the 19th century, create an immersive experience that is appropriate to its holdings, yet speaks to today’s museum visitor?

—

Featured image: A poem snakes around the artworks on a wall dedicated to nightmares.
The exhibition architecture is designed by Lauri Johansson
Photo: Finnish National Gallery / Aleks Talve

title: Diaries as a Primary Source for Understanding Lauri Anttila’s World
content:
Dr Marja Sakari, Director Emerita, Ateneum Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery

—

Diary: ‘A form of autobiographical writing, a regularly kept record of the diarist’s activities and reflections. Written primarily for the writer’s use alone, the diary has a frankness that is unlike writing done for publication […].’[1]

Lauri Anttila (1938–2022), the Finnish pioneer of conceptual and environmental art, kept diaries throughout his professional life. The first date from the end of the 1960s and the last were written in 2022, just before he passed away. On many occasions he mentioned in his diaries that his aim was to publish part of them as an autobiography. With his wish in mind, I have used his diaries as source material for my own research to write a biography of the artist.
snippet: Dr Marja Sakari, Director Emerita, Ateneum Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery

title: Thoughts on Disintegrating Artwork – Artistic Research, Museum Work, and the Categories of Art
content:
Dr Saara Hacklin, Chief Curator, collections, Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma / Finnish National Gallery

This article has been originally written for In line, online, off the grid – Linjassa, linjoilla, katveessa, edited by Mika Elo & Maiju Loukola, Uniarts Helsinki, 2025, 271–75. The bilingual publication is available online https://urn.fi/URN:ISBN:978-952-353-489-6.

—

The main class describes the artwork with its common noun, for instance painting, photography, object art. The artwork can only have one main class, which is chosen primarily from the word list in the guidelines.[1]

I am writing this article from (at least) two viewpoints. On the one hand, I am drawing from my experiences as an examiner of artistic research: I was part of the external examination board of the Academy of Fine Arts, Uniarts Helsinki, from 2016 until 2024, and in addition I have examined artistic doctoral research projects at other institutions. For several years I have also been a full-time curator of contemporary art and in particular working with the collections of the Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma at the Finnish National Gallery.

title: Light Weight, A Baroque Way of Curating – Edith Karlson’s Sculptures in Exhibition
content:
Piia Oksanen, MA, Curator, Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma / Finnish National Gallery

—

Weight

It is on point to begin this text about the exhibition ‘Edith Karlson: Dawn till Dawn’ at the Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma by focusing on materials, on the very concrete. Karlson works hands-on with a variety of materials. In her practice the materials retain their characteristics, they are still able to communicate their origins, even when sculpted and given form to convey other narratives. Karlson seems to stop working at the point where the material can still hold onto its essence. Clay, concrete, latex; mostly monochromatic, mainly bare, tactile.
snippet:
Piia Oksanen, MA, Curator, Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma / Finnish National Gallery

title: Sámi Art Speaks Out at Kiasma
content:
Gill Crabbe, FNG Research

The exhibition of Sámi art ‘We Who Remain’, now open at the Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma in Helsinki, is timely politically, as well as artistically. Gill Crabbe gives an overview of the show and interviews the independent curator Petra Laiti about her collaboration with the museum

—

A major exhibition of Sámi art at the Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma in Helsinki, brings together contemporary artworks by Sámi artists and works engaging with Sámi life across Sápmi, spanning Finland, Sweden, and Norway. The exhibition, ‘We Who Remain – Sámi Art in Focus’, which is jointly presented by Kiasma and the Sámi Museum Siida in Inari, is especially timely, following the recent report of Finland’s Sámi Truth and Reconciliation Commission, which includes recommendations to improve the status of the Sámi people and underlines Finland’s responsibility as a country founded on the homelands of two peoples.

title: The Finnish Art Society’s 180th Anniversary Symposium ‘Constructed Nationality: Transitions and Forces of Change in Visual Arts’ – The History of the Finnish Art Field
content:
Anna Luhtala, MA, Curator, Finnish National Gallery

—

The Finnish Art Society’s 180th Anniversary Symposium was held on 22 April, 2026, at the Ateneum Art Museum. In the opening speech, Outi Raatikainen, the Society’s chairperson, described how the founding document of the Finnish Art Society was signed in 1846, and two years later, the art school was founded and a grants system and the Ducat Prize established to support Finnish artists. ‘The Finnish Art Society gathered artists, collectors, networkers, and influencers – all for the arts’, Raatikainen stated. Each year, the Finnish Art Society awards grants to artists, buys artworks to be donated to the members for the annual lottery event and guarantees access to art for the members by offering free entrance to its exhibitions. ‘In future’, Raatikainen said, ‘the society wants to do even more, and this year, a fundraising campaign will be launched and a gala event for donors is to be held in October.’

—

Featured image: Students from the Finnish Art Society’s Drawing School in the painting studio at the Ateneum in 1899. Photographer Jakob Ljungqvist.
Finnish National Gallery Collection, Väinö Hämäläinen Archive

title: Editorial: Redefining ‘the Artist’ through New Narratives
content:
Dr Anna-Maria von Bonsdorff, Director, Ateneum Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery

25 March 2026

Finnish art is now more visible and present internationally than ever. Museums, such as New York’s Metropolitan Museum of Art, are acquiring Finnish art and Finnish art is present in many exhibitions in major museums around Europe and the US. This interest is growing. However, it also means that, as museum professionals and curators at the Finnish National Gallery, we are facing the fact that it is even more important that knowledge of Finnish history is expertly disseminated to our counterparts abroad, in addition to explaining the context the artists were and are working in. It also means a shift away from the emphasis on the solitary genius artist.
snippet:
Dr Anna-Maria von Bonsdorff, Director, Ateneum Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery

title: Conservation of Four Sketches, by Akseli Gallen-Kallela, for the Frescoes at the Finnish Pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair
content:
Ilona Osara BA, Paper Conservator, Finnish National Gallery

—

In 1898, three Finnish architects, Eliel Saarinen, Armas Lindgren and Herman Gesellius won the competition to design Finland’s pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair. Since 1896, Akseli Gallen-Kallela (1865–1931) had been creating paintings inspired by the Kalevala, the Finnish national epic, each work contributing to the emerging Finnish national identity. He was commissioned to paint murals for the Finnish Pavilion’s Iris Room and to decorate the arches of the pavilion, giving him an opportunity to present his art to an international audience.[1]

The Finnish Pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair became a landmark project that showcased Finland as a modern and distinct nation. Featuring the finest Finnish art, design, and industry, it earned widespread acclaim.[2] Gallen-Kallela himself was awarded a gold medal by the World’s Fair for his contributions.[3] 

—
snippet: Ilona Osara BA, Paper Conservator, Finnish National Gallery
